You are a vPlan generator.

Create a vPlan table from the provided JSON requirements.

Rules:
- Use only the information in the input JSON.
- Do not invent missing technical details.
- Do not include requirement_text or requirement_description. These will be added later from the input requirements file.
- Every requirement must have at least one test.
- A requirement may have multiple tests if it contains multiple independent behaviours, modes, bit fields, causes, outcomes, reset cases, or software-visible conditions.
- If a requirement contains multiple status bits, command bits, encoded values, or transfer directions, split by each bit, value, or direction.
- Each test must verify one main behaviour only.
- Include negative tests only where the requirement defines a disabled state, forbidden condition, invalid access, error condition, or non-happy-path behaviour.
- Do not invent negative tests if the input JSON does not imply a valid negative scenario.
- Do not split a test just because multiple signals are involved in the same behaviour.
- Multiple rows may share the same requirement_id.
- Each row must have a unique test_id.
- Keep test_steps to 1-3 concise steps.
- Keep expected_results to 1-2 concise results.

Test types:
- positive = normal valid behaviour, including valid modes, valid encoded values, and expected state transitions.
- negative = disabled, invalid, forbidden, error, access-violation, or non-happy-path behaviour.
- Do not mark an opposite valid encoded value as negative unless it represents an error, disabled state, invalid access, or forbidden behaviour.

Required columns:
test_id, requirement_id, test_type, test_description, test_constraints, test_steps, expected_results, priority, coverage.

Coverage:
- covered = directly testable from the provided information
- partially_covered = only partly testable from the provided information
- blocked = not usefully testable from the provided information

Priority:
- 1 = high priority functional/protocol/register/reset/interrupt behaviour
- 2 = medium priority integration or externally dependent behaviour
- 3 = lower priority software guidance or unclear hardware enforcement

This flag represents acknowledge from the addressed slave. ‘1’ = No acknowledge received. ‘0’ = Acknowledge received.","source_section":"3.2.6 Status register","signals":["SR[7]","RxACK","SDA"],"type":"status_requirement"},{"id":"REQ_I2C_038","description":"BUSY must indicate whether the I2C bus is busy, setting after START detection and clearing after STOP detection.","text":"6 | R | Busy, I2C bus busy. ‘1’ after START signal detected. ‘0’ after STOP signal detected.","source_section":"3.2.6 Status register","signals":["SR[6]","BUSY","SCL","SDA"],"type":"status_requirement"},{"id":"REQ_I2C_039","description":"AL must indicate arbitration lost.","text":"5 | R | AL, Arbitration lost. This bit is set when the core lost arbitration.","source_section":"3.2.6 Status register","signals":["SR[5]","AL","SCL","SDA"],"type":"arbitration_requirement"},{"id":"REQ_I2C_040","description":"Arbitration must be considered lost when a STOP signal is detected but was not requested.","text":"Arbitration is lost when: a STOP signal is detected, but non requested","source_section":"3.2.6 Status register","signals":["AL","STO","SCL","SDA"],"type":"arbitration_requirement"},{"id":"REQ_I2C_041","description":"Arbitration must be considered lost when the master drives SDA high but SDA is low.","text":"Arbitration is lost when: The master drives SDA high, but SDA is low.","source_section":"3.2.6 Status register","signals":["AL","SDA","sda_pad_o","sda_pad_i"],"type":"arbitration_requirement"},{"id":"REQ_I2C_042","description":"TIP must indicate whether a transfer is in progress.","text":"1 | R | TIP, Transfer in progress. ‘1’ when transferring data. ‘0’ when transfer complete.","source_section":"3.2.6 Status register","signals":["SR[1]","TIP"],"type":"status_requirement"},{"id":"REQ_I2C_043","description":"IF must indicate whether an interrupt is pending.","text":"0 | R | IF, Interrupt Flag. This bit is set when an interrupt is pending","source_section":"3.2.6 Status register","signals":["SR[0]","IF"],"type":"interrupt_requirement"},{"id":"REQ_I2C_044","description":"A pending interrupt must cause a processor interrupt request when IEN is set.","text":"This bit is set when an interrupt is pending, which will cause a processor interrupt request if the IEN bit is set.","source_section":"3.2.6 Status register","signals":["IF","IEN","wb_inta_o"],"type":"interrupt_requirement"},{"id":"REQ_I2C_045","description":"IF must be set when one byte transfer has completed or arbitration is lost.","text":"The Interrupt Flag is set when: one byte transfer has been completed; arbitration is lost","source_section":"3.2.6 Status register","signals":["IF","SR[0]","TIP","AL"],"type":"interrupt_requirement"},{"id":"REQ_I2C_046","description":"The Status Register reset value must be 0x00.","text":"Reset Value: 0x00","source_section":"3.2.6 Status register","signals":["SR"],"type":"reset_requirement"},{"id":"REQ_I2C_047","description":"Reserved bits must read as zero, and writable reserved bits should be written as zero.","text":"Please note that all reserved bits are read as zeros. To ensure forward compatibility, they should be written as zeros.","source_section":"3.2.6 Status register","signals":["SR[4:2]","CR[2:1]","CTR[5:0]"],"type":"reserved_bit_requirement"},{"id":"REQ_I2C_048","description":"Data must be transferred between a Master and Slave synchronously to SCL on SDA, byte by byte.","text":"Data is transferred between a Master and a Slave synchronously to SCL on the SDA line on a byte-by-byte basis.","source_section":"4.1 System Configuration","signals":["SCL","SDA"],"type":"protocol_rule"},{"id":"REQ_I2C_049","description":"Each I2C data byte must be 8 bits long and transmitted most significant bit first.","text":"Each data byte is 8 bits long. There is one SCL clock pulse for each data bit with the MSB being transmitted first.","source_section":"4.1 System Configuration","signals":["SCL","SDA"],"type":"protocol_rule"},{"id":"REQ_I2C_050","description":"Each transferred byte must be followed by an acknowledge bit on the ninth SCL clock cycle.","text":"An acknowledge bit follows each transferred byte. Each transferred byte is followed by an acknowledge bit on the 9th SCL clock cycle.","source_section":"4.1 System Configuration / 4.2.3 Data Transfer","signals":["SDA","SCL","ACK","RxACK"],"type":"timing_requirement"},{"id":"REQ_I2C_051","description":"Data bits must be sampled during SCL high, and SDA must remain stable while SCL is high during data transfer.","text":"Each bit is sampled during the high period of SCL. The SDA line may be changed only during the low period of SCL and must be held stable during the high period of SCL.","source_section":"4.1 System Configuration","signals":["SDA","SCL"],"type":"timing_requirement"},{"id":"REQ_I2C_052","description":"A standard I2C communication must follow the sequence START, slave address transfer, data transfer, and STOP.","text":"Normally, a standard communication consists of four parts: 1) START signal generation 2) Slave address transfer 3) Data transfer 4) STOP signal generation","source_section":"4.2 I2C Protocol","signals":["SDA","SCL"],"type":"protocol_sequence_requirement"},{"id":"REQ_I2C_053","description":"A START signal must be a high-to-low transition of SDA while SCL is high.","text":"A START signal, usually referred to as the S-bit, is defined as a high-to-low transition of SDA while SCL is high.","source_section":"4.2.1 START signal","signals":["SDA","SCL"],"type":"protocol_rule"},{"id":"REQ_I2C_054","description":"A Repeated START must be a START signal generated without first generating a STOP signal.","text":"A Repeated START is a START signal without first generating a STOP signal.","source_section":"4.2.1 START signal","signals":["SDA","SCL"],"type":"protocol_rule"},{"id":"REQ_I2C_055","description":"The core must generate a START signal when STA is set and either RD or WR is set.","text":"The core generates a START signal when the STA-bit in the Command Register is set and the RD or WR bits are set.","source_section":"4.2.1 START signal","signals":["STA","RD","WR","SDA","SCL"],"type":"command_requirement"},{"id":"REQ_I2C_056","description":"A STOP signal must be a low-to-high transition of SDA while SCL is high.","text":"A STOP signal, usually referred to as the P-bit, is defined as a low-to-high transition of SDA while SCL is at logical ‘1’.","source_section":"4.2.4 STOP signal","signals":["SDA","SCL"],"type":"protocol_rule"},{"id":"REQ_I2C_057","description":"The first byte transferred by the master after a START signal must be the slave address with a seven-bit address followed by an RW bit.","text":"The first byte of data transferred by the master immediately after the START signal is the slave address. This is a seven-bits calling address followed by a RW bit.","source_section":"4.2.2 Slave Address Transfer","signals":["SDA","SCL","RW"],"type":"protocol_sequence_requirement"},{"id":"REQ_I2C_058","description":"The addressed slave must acknowledge by pulling SDA low at the ninth SCL clock cycle.","text":"Only the slave with an address that matches the one transmitted by the master will respond by returning an acknowledge bit by pulling the SDA low at the 9th SCL clock cycle.","source_section":"4.2.2 Slave Address Transfer","signals":["SDA","SCL","ACK"],"type":"timing_requirement"},{"id":"REQ_I2C_059","description":"The core must support 10-bit slave addresses by generating two address transfers.","text":"The core supports 10bit slave addresses by generating two address transfers.","source_section":"4.2.2 Slave Address Transfer","signals":["SDA","SCL"],"type":"protocol_sequence_requirement"},{"id":"REQ_I2C_060","description":"The core must treat a slave address transfer as a write action using the Transmit Register and WR bit.","text":"The core treats a Slave Address Transfer as any other write action. Store the slave device’s address in the Transmit Register and set the WR bit. The core will then transfer the slave address on the bus.","source_section":"4.2.2 Slave Address Transfer","signals":["TXR","WR","SDA","SCL"],"type":"command_requirement"},{"id":"REQ_I2C_061","description":"After successful slave addressing, data transfer must proceed byte by byte in the direction specified by the RW bit.","text":"Once successful slave addressing has been achieved, the data transfer can proceed on a byte-by-byte basis in the direction specified by the RW bit sent by the master.","source_section":"4.2.3 Data Transfer","signals":["SDA","SCL","RW"],"type":"protocol_sequence_requirement"},{"id":"REQ_I2C_062","description":"If the slave signals No Acknowledge, the master may generate a STOP or Repeated START signal.","text":"If the slave signals a No Acknowledge, the master can generate a STOP signal to abort the data transfer or generate a Repeated START signal and start a new transfer cycle.","source_section":"4.2.3 Data Transfer","signals":["SDA","SCL","STO","STA","RxACK"],"type":"protocol_rule"},{"id":"REQ_I2C_063","description":"To write data to a slave, software must store the data in the Transmit Register and set the WR bit.","text":"To write data to a slave, store the data to be transmitted in the Transmit Register and set the WR bit.","source_section":"4.2.3 Data Transfer","signals":["TXR","WR"],"type":"software_usage_rule"},{"id":"REQ_I2C_064","description":"To read data from a slave, software must set the RD bit.","text":"To read data from a slave, set the RD bit.","source_section":"4.2.3 Data Transfer","signals":["RD"],"type":"software_usage_rule"},{"id":"REQ_I2C_065","description":"When a transfer is done, the core must reset TIP and set IF.","text":"When the transfer is done the TIP flag is reset, the IF flag set","source_section":"4.2.3 Data Transfer","signals":["TIP","IF"],"type":"status_requirement"},{"id":"REQ_I2C_066","description":"When interrupt generation is enabled and a transfer is done, the core must generate an interrupt.","text":"When the transfer is done the TIP flag is reset, the IF flag set and, when enabled, an interrupt generated.","source_section":"4.2.3 Data Transfer","signals":["IF","IEN","wb_inta_o"],"type":"interrupt_requirement"},{"id":"REQ_I2C_067","description":"The Receive Register must contain valid data after IF has been set.","text":"The Receive Register contains valid data after the IF flag has been set.","source_section":"4.2.3 Data Transfer","signals":["RXR","IF"],"type":"data_validity_requirement"},{"id":"REQ_I2C_068","description":"Software may issue a new read or write command when TIP is reset.","text":"The user may issue a new write or read command when the TIP flag is reset.","source_section":"4.2.3 Data Transfer","signals":["RD","WR","TIP"],"type":"software_usage_rule"},{"id":"REQ_I2C_069","description":"When two or more masters try to control the bus simultaneously, clock synchronization must determine the bus clock.","text":"If two or more masters simultaneously try to control the bus, a clock synchronization procedure determines the bus clock.","source_section":"4.3.1 Clock Synchronization","signals":["SCL"],"type":"clock_synchronization_requirement"},{"id":"REQ_I2C_070","description":"During clock synchronization, SCL must be held low by the device with the longest low period and held high by the device with the shortest high period.","text":"the SCL line will therefore be held low by the device with the longest low period and held high by the device with the shortest high period.","source_section":"4.3.1 Clock Synchronization","signals":["SCL"],"type":"clock_synchronization_requirement"},{"id":"REQ_I2C_071","description":"Slave devices may slow the transfer bit rate by holding SCL low after the master has driven SCL low.","text":"After the master has driven SCL low, the slave can drive SCL low for the required period and then release it.","source_section":"4.3.2 Clock Stretching","signals":["SCL"],"type":"clock_stretching_requirement"},{"id":"REQ_I2C_072","description":"If the slave SCL low period is greater than the master SCL low period, the resulting SCL bus low period must be stretched and wait states inserted.","text":"If the slave’s SCL low period is greater than the master’s SCL low period, the resulting SCL bus signal low period is stretched, thus inserting wait-states.","source_section":"4.3.2 Clock Stretching","signals":["SCL"],"type":"clock_stretching_requirement"}]
